primary_diagnosis.name: Acute cellulitis of the right lower leg with systemic features (fever and tachycardia) on a background of institutional mistrust and clinical-environment anxiety driving delayed presentation

comorbidities.name: Adjustment reaction / healthcare access anxiety with institutional mistrust (situational clinical-environment panic)

pathophysiology_summary: A breach of the skin barrier (a minor scrape) has allowed bacteria (commonly Streptococcus pyogenes or Staphylococcus aureus) to seed the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, producing spreading cellulitis: erythema, warmth, induration and pain. Over a week of untreated progression the infection has produced a systemic inflammatory response (fever 38.2, tachycardia) — the pathway to bacteraemia and sepsis if antibiotics are delayed further. The situational anxiety and institutional mistrust are a real and valid overlay that drove the dangerous care delay and inflate the measured heart rate, but they are the CONTEXT, not the explanation for the fever, the spreading erythema or the induration. Remotely, cellulitis cannot be distinguished with certainty from deep vein thrombosis or an early necrotising soft-tissue infection, and sepsis severity cannot be graded without examination and bloods — hence in-person assessment is mandatory.

differential.evidence_basis: One-week spreading, hot, tender erythema from a skin breach, now with subjective fever and palpitations.
Unilateral hot, swollen, painful lower leg — cannot be excluded remotely; needs examination and Doppler.

differential.diagnosis: situational anxiety / panic as the sole explanation
differential.evidence_basis: Anxiety cannot produce a spreading, hot, indurated area of erythema or an objective fever; treating this as 'just anxiety' is the dismissive-under-triage trap.

differential.diagnosis: cellulitis with systemic features / early sepsis risk
differential.evidence_basis: Fever plus tachycardia plus a spreading infection untreated for a week meets systemic-inflammatory criteria — time-critical for antibiotics and a sepsis screen.
Exquisite pain out of proportion and rapid progression are red flags; can only be excluded on urgent in-person assessment.
Does not explain a systemic fever, marked induration or deep tenderness; unilateral spread from a scrape argues against it.

differential.diagnosis: acute cellulitis of the lower leg with systemic features
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed on in-person examination (15 x 10 cm erythema, warmth, induration, tenderness, fever 38.2); DVT and necrotising infection excluded clinically; the anxiety is a valid comorbid driver of the delay, not the cause of the infection.

red_flags.description: Spreading erythema with a systemic fever (38.2) — evolving cellulitis with systemic involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features on top of a spreading skin infection signal a real risk of progression to bacteraemia and sepsis; antibiotics and a sepsis screen are time-critical.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated progression to systemic sepsis and potential death — the exact outcome the source note was trying to prevent.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia and feeling systemically unwell — possible early sepsis physiology
red_flags.clinical_significance: A raised heart rate with fever is a systemic-inflammatory-response marker; it must not be dismissed as anxiety when a spreading infection is present.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'anxiety' and under-triaging a septic process.

red_flags.description: Deliberate one-week delay and ongoing avoidance of care due to institutional mistrust
red_flags.clinical_significance: The avoidance is why the infection reached systemic features and is the single biggest driver of preventable harm; the encounter must engage the mistrust so she accepts assessment rather than refusing it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Coercion or dismissal triggers total care refusal and untreated sepsis.

red_flags.description: Unilateral hot, swollen, painful lower leg — deep vein thrombosis cannot be excluded remotely
red_flags.clinical_significance: DVT shares this presentation and carries a pulmonary-embolism risk; only in-person examination and imaging can exclude it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed DVT progressing to pulmonary embolism.

red_flags.description: Exquisite pain out of proportion with rapid spread — necrotising soft-tissue infection must be considered
red_flags.clinical_significance: Necrotising infection is a surgical emergency; pain out of proportion and rapid progression are its early telehealth-visible warning signs.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of a limb- and life-threatening surgical emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling the presentation 'just anxiety' or 'just a rash' because of the mistrust/panic framing, and under-triaging a spreading systemic infection. This is the prime error the case is built to catch.
Accepting that the racing heart is 'only anxiety' and therefore missing the fever + tachycardia sepsis physiology.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failure to consider DVT / necrotising infection
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a unilateral hot swollen leg as cellulitis-only and not flagging that DVT and necrotising infection can only be excluded in person.
Attempting to diagnose and prescribe oral antibiotics remotely without examination or a sepsis screen, instead of arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment.
Ordering the patient to an emergency department in a way that ignores her mistrust, prompting her to refuse all care — the failure mode the source home visit was designed to avoid.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the leg for induration, fluctuance, crepitus or the true extent of erythema
Accurate blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and temperature
Assessment for deep vein thrombosis (examination and Doppler ultrasound)
Blood tests (white cell count, CRP, lactate, blood cultures) and formal sepsis scoring
Whether an early necrotising soft-tissue infection is present
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Validate that the infection is real and serious, and arrange URGENT same-day in-person medical assessment (GP, urgent care, or an after-hours home-visit service) for examination, antibiotics and a sepsis screen. Escalate immediately to 000 / an emergency department if sepsis red flags emerge (rigors, confusion, faintness, rapidly spreading redness, fever above 39, breathlessness, or dusky/blistering skin). Engage the mistrust with concrete accommodations rather than dismissing or coercing.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real danger is UNDER-action: dismissive psychosocial closure — attributing the tachycardia and distress to anxiety and the leg to a benign rash, and failing to escalate a spreading systemic infection. Premature reassurance here can be fatal.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cellulitis and erysipelas (eTG, Australia)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Cellulitis with systemic features or rapid spread warrants urgent assessment; consider intravenous therapy and admission where systemic involvement or sepsis risk is present.
Fever with tachycardia and a source of infection should prompt structured sepsis recognition and time-critical escalation.
